hints: A measure of a force's tendency to cause rotation
Car engines are rated by it — more of it means better acceleration from a stop
Calculated as force multiplied by the perpendicular distance from the pivot point
Measured in newton-metres (N·m) or foot-pounds (ft·lb)
Formula: τ = r × F, where r is the moment arm and F is the applied force
An internal force per unit area within a material
What engineers calculate to check whether a part will break under load
Two main types: normal (perpendicular to surface) and shear (parallel)
Measured in pascals (Pa) or megapascals (MPa)
Formula: σ = F / A, where F is force and A is cross-sectional area
A phenomenon where a system vibrates at maximum amplitude
The Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapsed in 1940 partly because of it
Occurs when the driving frequency matches the system's natural frequency
Used intentionally in musical instruments to amplify sound
Engineers deliberately design structures to avoid it — or use dampers to suppress it
A type of material failure caused by repeated loading and unloading
Responsible for roughly 80% of all mechanical failures in service
A crack starts small, grows invisibly with each cycle, then suddenly causes total fracture
The 1954 de Havilland Comet crashes were a landmark case of this failure mode
Characterized by an S-N (Wöhler) curve plotting stress amplitude vs cycles to failure
The branch of physics dealing with heat, work, and energy
Its laws govern everything from steam engines to refrigerators
The second law says entropy of an isolated system always increases
Four laws: zeroth (thermal equilibrium), first (energy conservation), second (entropy), third (absolute zero)
The Carnot cycle sets the theoretical maximum efficiency for a heat engine operating between two temperatures
A force that resists relative motion between surfaces in contact
Without it, you couldn't walk, drive, or grip anything
Static type is greater than kinetic — it takes more force to start sliding than to keep sliding
Characterized by a dimensionless coefficient (μ) specific to each surface pair
Formula: F = μN, where N is the normal (perpendicular) force between the surfaces
The stress at which a material begins to deform permanently
Below this point, remove the load and the part springs back to its original shape
Above it, the material plastically deforms — it won't return to its original dimensions
A key property engineers use to set safety margins for structural components
Identified on a stress-strain curve as the point where the linear (elastic) region ends
The relationship between the rotational speeds of two meshing gears
A bicycle's multiple gears are everyday examples of choosing it for different needs
A higher ratio multiplies torque but reduces output speed
Calculated as the number of teeth on the driven gear divided by the number on the drive gear
Used in transmissions to match engine speed to wheel speed across a range of driving conditions
A fundamental concept in fluid dynamics
Explains why airplane wings generate lift
States that faster-moving fluid has lower pressure
Also explains the curveball in baseball and why shower curtains blow inward
Derived from conservation of energy: as fluid speeds up, its pressure drops to compensate
A measure of deformation relative to original size
The companion quantity to stress — together they define how materials behave under load
Dimensionless — expressed as a ratio or percentage
Elastic strain is recoverable; plastic strain is permanent
Formula: ε = ΔL / L₀, where ΔL is the change in length and L₀ is the original length
A device for transferring heat between two fluids without mixing them
Your car radiator is a simple example
Used in power plants, HVAC systems, and chemical refineries
Common designs include shell-and-tube, plate-and-frame, and finned tube
Effectiveness is measured by how closely the outlet temperatures approach each other relative to the inlet temperature difference
A body's resistance to changes in rotational motion
The rotational analogue of mass in linear mechanics
Depends not just on how much mass an object has, but on how far that mass is from the axis of rotation
Why spinning figure skaters pull in their arms to spin faster — reducing it increases angular velocity
For a point mass: I = mr²; for extended bodies, it's the integral of r² dm over the whole object
A fluid's resistance to flow
Honey has high viscosity; water has low viscosity
Lubricating oils are rated by it — engine oil labels like 10W-40 describe it at different temperatures
Dynamic viscosity is measured in pascal-seconds (Pa·s) or centipoise (cP)
Newtonian fluids have constant viscosity regardless of shear rate; non-Newtonian fluids (like ketchup) do not
A sketch showing all forces acting on an isolated object
The most fundamental tool in statics and dynamics
Forces shown include weight, normal force, friction, tension, and any applied loads
Drawing one is usually the first step in solving any mechanics problem
Once complete, Newton's second law (ΣF = ma) is applied to find unknowns
A rotating or sliding piece that converts rotational motion into linear or oscillatory motion
Found in every internal combustion engine, controlling the intake and exhaust valves
Its profile (shape) determines the exact motion of the component it drives
The component it drives is called a follower
Cam timing in engines (valve timing) is critical to performance and is a major tuning parameter
A fundamental law describing elastic behavior
States that deformation is proportional to the applied force — within limits
Named after 17th-century English scientist Robert Hooke
The constant of proportionality is called the spring constant (k)
Formula: F = kx, where F is force and x is displacement from equilibrium
A machine element that constrains relative motion between parts
Found in virtually every rotating machine — from skateboards to jet turbines
Reduces friction and supports loads between moving and stationary parts
Main types: ball, roller, tapered roller, and plain (sleeve) bearings
Selection depends on load direction (radial vs axial), speed, and environmental conditions
Force per unit area
Measured in pascals (Pa), bar, psi, or atmospheres depending on context
Tyre pressure, blood pressure, and atmospheric pressure all refer to it
Gauge pressure is measured relative to atmospheric; absolute pressure is relative to vacuum
Formula: P = F / A — the same formula as normal stress, but applied to fluids
The rate at which work is done or energy is transferred
Car engines and electric motors are rated in it
Measured in watts (W) or horsepower (hp) — 1 hp ≈ 746 W
Higher values mean the same work is done in less time
Formula: P = W / t = F·v, where W is work, t is time, F is force, and v is velocity
The permissible range of variation in a physical dimension
Every machined part has these — nothing is made to a perfect single value
Too tight and manufacturing becomes impossibly expensive; too loose and parts don't function
Defined as the difference between upper and lower acceptable limits
ISO and ANSI systems define standard fits (clearance, interference, transition) using these
A permanent joint formed by fusing materials, usually metals, together
Done by melting the base material — sometimes with a filler rod added
Common processes: MIG, TIG, stick (SMAW), and spot welding
Quality is assessed by visual inspection, ultrasonic testing, or X-ray
The heat-affected zone (HAZ) around the joint changes the microstructure and can weaken or harden the material
The force directed toward the center of a circular path
Without it, an object moving in a circle would fly off in a straight line
What keeps satellites in orbit and cars on curved roads
Commonly confused with centrifugal force — which is a fictitious force in the rotating reference frame
Formula: F = mv²/r, where m is mass, v is speed, and r is the radius of curvature
A sudden sideways deflection of a slender structural element under compressive load
The failure mode that collapsed thin-walled columns long before the material reaches its yield strength
Why structural members are often made hollow or with I-shaped cross-sections rather than solid rods
Euler derived the critical load formula for columns in 1744
Critical load: P_cr = π²EI / (KL)², where E is Young's modulus, I is moment of inertia, L is length, K is end-condition factor
The ratio of useful output to total input
Always less than 100% — some energy is always lost, usually as heat
A car engine converts roughly 20–40% of fuel energy into motion; the rest is waste heat
Expressed as a percentage: (useful output / total input) × 100
The second law of thermodynamics sets an absolute ceiling called the Carnot efficiency
The branch of physics studying the behavior of liquids and gases
Covers everything from pipe flow to aircraft aerodynamics to ocean currents
Divided into fluid statics (at rest) and fluid dynamics (in motion)
The Navier-Stokes equations govern fluid motion — they're notoriously difficult to solve
Reynolds number predicts whether flow is laminar (smooth) or turbulent (chaotic)
Three fundamental principles describing the relationship between forces and motion
First published in Principia Mathematica in 1687
First law: objects stay at rest or in motion unless acted on by a net force
Second law: F = ma — force equals mass times acceleration
Third law: for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
A rotating machine element that transmits torque and motion
Found in virtually every mechanical drivetrain
Must be designed against torsion, bending, and fatigue failure
Key connections: keyways, splines, and press fits attach components like gears and pulleys to it
Critical speed — the rotational frequency that excites resonance — is a key design constraint
The interdisciplinary field studying the structure, properties, and processing of materials
Covers metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites
Microstructure — grain size, phase distribution — determines macroscopic mechanical properties
Heat treatment processes (annealing, quenching, tempering) alter material properties deliberately
The structure-property-processing-performance paradigm is its central framework
An elastic machine element that stores and releases mechanical energy
Found in everything from ballpoint pens to vehicle suspensions
Obeys Hooke's Law within its elastic range
Types include compression, extension, torsion, and leaf springs
Spring rate (stiffness, k) is the key design parameter — measured in N/m or lb/in
A material's resistance to surface indentation or scratching
Not the same as strength — a material can be hard but brittle
Measured using standardized tests: Rockwell, Brinell, Vickers, and Mohs scales
High hardness improves wear resistance, which is why cutting tools are hardened
In metals, it correlates roughly with tensile strength — Brinell hardness × 3.45 ≈ UTS in MPa
